coredata.dc:description: This study focuses on the synthesis of SiO2-coated carbon dots (CDs) grafted onto YAlO3:Cr3+ (YAP:Cr3+) nanophosphors (NPs) through a combustion method. The CDs enhance the absorption cross-section of Cr3+ ions, significantly boosting the luminescence of YAP:Cr3+ by 19.29-fold. Further improvement is achieved with a SiO2 coating, leading to a 48.73-fold increase in photoluminescence intensity. The NCs are colour tunable, with emissions ranging from pinkish-red to blue, depending on the concentration of CDs and the excitation wavelength. These properties make them suitable for diverse applications, such as ratio metric fluorescence sensors for detecting Fe3+ ions within the 0–350 µM range, with high sensitivity (0.2 µM) and a low detection limit (4.93 μM). Additionally, they serve as material for white light-emitting diodes (W-LEDs), achieving CIE coordinates of (0.31, 0.30) and a rendering index of 94. The NCs also function as an optical thermometer with a sensitivity of 0.31 % K−1 at 320 K, making them ideal for temperature sensing applications. Moreover, their stability and dual-emissive nature make them highly effective in latent fingerprints (LFPs) detection, enabling high-resolution, level III fingerprint analysis. The dual emission properties of CDs and Cr3+ ions position these NCs as multifunctional materials, suitable for optical sensing, lighting, and security applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study focuses on the synthesis of SiO2-coated carbon dots (CDs) grafted onto YAlO3:Cr3+ (YAP:Cr3+) nanophosphors (NPs) through a combustion method. The CDs enhance the absorption cross-section of Cr3+ ions, significantly boosting the luminescence of YAP:Cr3+ by 19.29-fold. Further improvement is achieved with a SiO2 coating, leading to a 48.73-fold increase in photoluminescence intensity. The NCs are colour tunable, with emissions ranging from pinkish-red to blue, depending on the concentration of CDs and the excitation wavelength. These properties make them suitable for diverse applications, such as ratio metric fluorescence sensors for detecting Fe3+ ions within the 0–350 µM range, with high sensitivity (0.2 µM) and a low detection limit (4.93 μM). Additionally, they serve as material for white light-emitting diodes (W-LEDs), achieving CIE coordinates of (0.31, 0.30) and a rendering index of 94. The NCs also function as an optical thermometer with a sensitivity of 0.31 % K−1 at 320 K, making them ideal for temperature sensing applications. Moreover, their stability and dual-emissive nature make them highly effective in latent fingerprints (LFPs) detection, enabling high-resolution, level III fingerprint analysis. The dual emission properties of CDs and Cr3+ ions position these NCs as multifunctional materials, suitable for optical sensing, lighting, and security applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Designing composites is an effective approach to enhancing optical properties [1]. For instance, by varying the quantity of CDs and Ln3+, Y. Wang and colleagues were able to effectively build CDs grafted YF3:RE3+ (RE = Eu, Tb) NCs by a simple hydrothermal method, achieving the near white light emission [2]. In comparison to CDs, the CDs@Cs2NaInCl6 NCs developed by W. Sun et al. exhibit green light emission with excellent stability under UV irradiation, heat, and humidity. The study concludes that combining CDs with double perovskites presents a novel strategy for designing white light-emitting phosphors, potentially leading to the development of advanced lighting solutions for the future [3]. White emitting hybrid for flexible film and W-LED applications was created by J. Y. Jung et al. through the synthesis of CDs impregnated in CaWO4:Eu3+ NCs [4].

Another appreciated lead-free perovskite, YAP:Cr3+, may produce multi-coloured emissions through ion doping, which is desirable for a high CRI. A host material with good optical, mechanical properties, and thermal comparable to YAG crystals is yttrium ortho-aluminate (YAlO3) [5]. YAP crystals are a multipurpose class of materials that find fundamental use as substrate materials for high-temperature superconductor thin films, laser technology, W-LEDs, scintillators, optical recording media, and optical thermometers, among other applications [6–8].

Since tissue equivalency is not a concern, YAP relatively high effective atomic number (Zeff = 31.4) makes it appropriate for certain applications. YAP has the ability to modify its laser emissions dramatically when doped with specific active transition elements, which presents plenty of benefits [9]. Mn4+ and Cr3+, two transition metal ions, are regarded as some of the most significant non-rare-earth red-emitting ions for usage in w-LEDs. The overall cost of producing w-LEDs is lowered by using these activator ions, which has major financial advantages. Cr3+-activated phosphors primarily emit light in the far-red and near-infrared (NIR) spectral regions, whereas Mn4+ ions are especially effective as phosphors emitting in the red region of visible light [10,11]. As shown in Table 1 , extensive research has been conducted on using SiO2 as a shell for phosphor cores [12–17]. SiO2 shells for radiant nanoparticles have a number of benefits, including as enhanced heat stability, better dispersibility of the particles, and surface functionalisation with different organic molecules. Coating SiO2 particles to the surface of phosphor nanoparticles offers a notable benefit by enhancing PL properties, and stability. This improvement is mostly due to the reduction of surface-related quenching effects on luminescence, which is achieved by reducing the number of surface hydroxyl (–OH) groups and defects [18]. W-LEDs are now widely used in the lighting industry due to their advantages over traditional lighting devices, such as compact size, long lifespan, low energy consumption, cost-effectiveness, and environmental friendliness [19]. The conversion of blue light generated by the LED chip into white light makes phosphor materials essential to W-LEDs. This conversion can be achieved by combining CDs with red-emitting phosphors, which are essential for producing vibrant luminescence due to their high brightness and suitable colour properties. For W-LED applications, W. Li et al. successfully incorporated CDs into CaAl12O19:Mn4+ hybrid nanoparticles by adjusting the excitation wavelength and the mass ratio of CDs to CaAl12O19 [20]. In this study, YAP:Cr3+ is considered a promising red-emitting component for multi-coloured luminescence due to its high brightness and chromaticity. Its excellent thermal stability and luminescence efficiency make it a potential phosphor material for W-LEDs.

One essential physiological factor that influences chemical and biological processes is temperature. Temperature sensing has seen a recent surge in interest in fluorescence-based techniques, such as those utilising polymers, semiconductor nanoparticles, and rare-earth ion-activated luminous materials [21]. Because it is non-invasive and resilient in strong electromagnetic fields, fluorescence-based temperature detection is exceptional. This technique relies on changes in luminescence with temperature, including variations in decay lifetime, energy, and intensity. Due to the time-consuming post-processing involved in computing luminescence lifespan, the intensity-based approach is more suited for real-time temperature monitoring. Luminescent materials include CDs, organic fluorescent dyes, semiconductor nano-crystals, and rare-earth (RE) ions [22,23]. In particular, luminous CDs are distinguished among fluorescent carbon materials and have attracted considerable interest due to their stability, non-toxicity, and environmental friendliness. They are notably used in applications such as temperature sensing and W-LEDs. CDs@CaAl2O4:Mn4+ NCs, operates in the 80–300 K range with a relative sensitivity of 0.18 % K−1,providing moderate sensitivity [20]. Furthermore, the phosphor CDs@ONFC/NH2, which incorporates CDs into an organic nanofiber composite with amine groups, operates from 243-383 K and achieves a sensitivity of 0.25 % K−1 [24]. These NCs show optimized temperature sensing capabilities and sensitivity compared to the host materials. Thus, our objective is to develop a composite with enhanced thermal sensitivity.

Because FPs are permanent and unique, FP analysis has been recognised as a very reliable means of human identification since the 19th century. Although most FPs are typically invisible to the naked eye, the secretions from the finger leave characteristic ridge patterns on the skin, known as LFPs. As a result, scientists have investigated a number of techniques for FP detection and identification, including as cyanoacrylate/iodine fuming, vacuum metal deposition, powder dusting, and fluorescent dye staining [25]. Due to their many benefits, which include inexpensive cost of production, exceptional photostability, good fluorescence property, high biocompatibility, and low toxicity, interest in NCs has increased recently [26]. Consequently, LEDs, fluorescent anti-counterfeiting, bioimaging, and fluorescent sensing are only a few of the applications that make extensive use of CDs composites [27]. Li et al., [28] synthesised silicon-doped CDs that show off strong blue emission, which they successfully used to visualise latent FPs level II in detail. Narwal et al., [29] prepared CDs added Y2TiWO8:Ce3+ NCs for thermal sensing and LFP applications. The NC exhibit superior relative sensitivity and in detailed level-III FP features when compare to bare Y2TiWO8:Ce3+ NPs.

Heavy metal ions have long posed significant concerns for environmental safety and public health due to their highly toxic nature, biological accumulation, and non-biodegradability [30]. Adverse health risks can increase with both direct and indirect exposure to heavy metal ions, whether short-term or long-term [31]. Common metal ions called ferric ions (Fe3+) are well known to be helpful trace elements that play a role in many essential metabolic processes, such as nerve conduction, oxygen transport, gene production, and osmotic pressure management [32]. However, it is important to note that Fe3+, despite being a common metal ion, can cause significant harm when present in excessive amounts. Elevated levels of Fe3+ in the body can cause severe health issues such anaemia, heart disease, hepatitis, neurological illnesses, Alzheimer's disease, and even cancer. As a result, there needs to be strict oversight over the amount of iron in drinking water, with a 0.3 mg/L maximum allowed limit [33]. Fe3+ detection must therefore be precise and selective. Fe3+ analysis has been reported using the following techniques: atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), electrochemical analysis, mass spectrometry, surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), and spectrophotometry [34]. Even if they produce correct results, they invariably require expensive instruments or require increased sensitivity. Ratio metric analysis is acknowledged as being more sensitive and economical than these techniques, as it does not require expensive instruments or intricate operating processes. As a result, numerous fluorescence-based methods have been developed for Fe(III) detection. Numerous fluorescent materials are used in these approaches, such as metal nanoclusters, CDs, metal–organic frameworks (MOFs), organic probes, and quantum dots [35]. Because carbon dot composites have dual PL properties, they are also utilised in Fe3+ sensing. Furthermore, because CDs typically exhibit single band emission, measurements made using this method are less reliable and convenient for use in sensing applications than fluorescence ratiometric methods, which can account for the calibrations of two emission bands and overcome the non-uniform probe distribution and inaccuracy caused by instrumental factors [36]. Therefore, it is still difficult to discover a feasible way to deal with these issues. Table 2 shows the comparison of detection method, range and LoD with other reported literature [37–43].

In this work, we explored a novel colour-tunable composite of CDs and YAP:Cr3+, coated with SiO2, for applications in optical thermometry, W-LEDs, Fe3+ sensing and LFP detection. To our knowledge, no prior studies have examined the impact of Cr3+ doping on YAP or on their NCs containing SiO2 and CDs. The synthesized hybrid phosphors are applicable in solid-state lighting, temperature sensing, and forensic sciences, including LFP detection. This study investigates the structure and morphology of the NCs, their elemental composition, PL properties, and the effects of temperature and concentration quenching. The research encompasses applications in temperature sensing, W-LEDs, and LFP detection.

Yttrium(III) nitrate hexahydrate (Y(NO3)3:6H2O) [99.9 %], Aluminum nitrate nonahydrate (Al(NO3)3:9H2O) [99.9 %], Urea (NH2CONH2) [99 %], Chromium(III) nitrate nonahydrate Cr(NO3)3:9H2O [99 %], Citric acid (HOC(COOH)(CH2COOH)2) [99 %], Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (HO2CCH2)2NCH2CH2N(CH2CO2H)2 [99 %] and Tetraethyl orthosilicate (Si(OC2H5)4) [99.9 %], were procured form Sigma Aldric, India. All the chemicals are used without further purification.

To synthesize Cr3+ doped YAP nanoparticles, aluminium and yttrium nitrates were heated at 400 °C in stoichiometric proportions with urea as the fuel. The procedure was as follows: in a Pyrex dish, nitrates, urea, and double-distilled water were evenly combined for approximately five minutes with a magnetic stirrer. After that, the Pyrex dish was put in a 400 °C pre-heated furnace. The mixture expanded and released large amounts of gas at lower temperatures due to dehydration and breakdown. Subsequently, the foam ignited spontaneously, resulting in a fluffy and voluminous final product. The entire process lasted 15 mins. After taking the dish out of the furnace, the frothy substance was powdered to a fine consistency. This powder was utilised for characterisation after it was calcined for three hours at 1000 °C. Fig. 1 a shows the synthesis pathway of YAP:Cr3+. The following is the equation of combustion synthesis:
(1) Y(NO3)3 + 2Al(NO3)3 + 16CO(NH2)2 + 2Cr(NO3)3 → 2YAlO3Cr + 16CO2 + 32H2O + 25 N2

Fig. 1b illustrates the synthesis of CDs. Typically, 10 mL of secondary distilled water is used to dissolve 2 mmol of citric acid (CA) and 8 mmol of ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA), which is then ultrasonicated for 30 min. After that, the mixture is put into a hydrothermal reactor that has a 25 mL polytetrafluoroethylene lining. It is then heated for 6 hrs at 170 °C. The reactor is subsequently allowed to cool down on its own. First, a 0.22 μm microporous filter is used to filter out any insoluble solid particles from the resultant solution. Following that, it undergoes a 48-hour dialysate in a dialysis bag (with a 1000 Da molecular weight cut-off). For later usage, the finished solution is kept in a refrigerator at 4 °C.

The NCs were synthesised by dissolving 0.5 g of YAP:3Cr3+ NPs in a CDs solution. The pH of the CDs solution (1, 2, 4, 10, and 20 wt%) was raised to 6.5 by adding HCl acid to ethanol (1 ml). The NPs were ultrasonically sonicated until a homogeneous gel was formed after the CD solutions were added. The obtained gel was vacuum-dried at 80 °C for 12 h. Subsequently, the xero-gel was ground into a powder to produce the final CDs/YAP:Cr3+ NCs. The preparation process for the silica-coated CDs/YAP:Cr3+ NCs followed the same procedure, with the addition of tetraethyl ortho-silicate (TEOS). Fig. 1c illustrates the synthesis of CDs/YAP:Cr3+ NCs.

The preparation of W-LEDs involved a series of steps aimed at creating a high-quality luminescent coating using a mixture of nanocomposites. The key materials used in this experiment were two different types of nanocomposites: SiO2@CDs(4 wt%)/YAP:Cr3+ NCs and CDs(4 wt%)/YAP:Cr3+ NCs. These nanocomposites were prepared separately and then incorporated into a silicone matrix, specifically PDMS, which served as the binder material to hold the nanocomposites in place. The preparation procedure began with accurately weighing the two types of nanocomposites to achieve a final composition containing 4 wt% CDs. The SiO2@CDs/YAP:Cr3+ nanocomposite and CDs/YAP:Cr3+ were thoroughly mixed with PDMS. The prepared mixture was applied as a coating onto a 395 nm UV-LED chip This curing process ensured that the W-LED structure was stable and durable, making it suitable for further testing and characterization.

SiO2@CDs(4 wt%) encapsulated within YAP:Cr3+ NCs were employed for the detection of a broad spectrum of metal ions, including Ag+, Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cr2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Li+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni+ and Zn2+. The detection was achieved through the fluorescence quenching mechanism. In a typical detection procedure, the SiO2@CDs(4 wt%)/YAP:Cr3+ NCs were dispersed in an aqueous solution at a concentration of 10 mg/mL. Following this, solutions containing different metal ions at a concentration of 400 µM were added to the dispersion. After ensuring that the solutions were thoroughly mixed, fluorescence measurements were taken. These measurements were performed using an excitation wavelength of 390 nm. In a separate experiment aimed at studying the detection sensitivity towards Fe3+ ions, a similar procedure was followed. A 10 mg/mL solution of the SiO2@CDs(4 wt%)/YAP:Cr3+ NCs was prepared, and Fe3+ ions were added at varying concentrations ranging from 0 to 350 µM. The fluorescence intensity of the solution was then measured, once again using an excitation wavelength of 390 nm, to determine the response of the NCs to different concentrations of Fe3+ ions.

A male volunteer, age 25, cleaned and patted his hands for a few minutes before running his finger over a variety of surfaces, such as marble, aluminium foil, stainless steel, coins, and compact discs. To make the FPs on these substrates visible, the NCs were applied with a soft feather brush. Excess powder was removed with airflow. Digital photographs of the FPs were captured using a Canon DSLR camera (EOS 100D) with a SIGMA MACRO 50 mm lens (F2.8, EXDG).

In this study, various advanced characterization techniques were used to analyze the synthesized materials. Powder X-ray Diffraction (XRD) was conducted using a Shimadzu PXRD-7000 equipped with CuKα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) to determine the crystal structure and phase purity. Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) with the Nicolet FT-IR-400 was employed to identify functional groups and bonding structures in the samples. The surface composition and oxidation states were examined via X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) using an ESCALABMKII spectrometer. Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) with a JEOL JSM-7000F Field Emission system provided detailed images of the sample's surface morphology. For higher resolution imaging at the nanoscale, Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) and High-Resolution TEM (HRTEM) were performed using a JEM-2010 to study particle size and crystallinity. The optical properties were investigated through Photoluminescence (PL) measurements using a Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluorolog-3 spectrofluorometer. Photoluminescence lifetime decay was measured with the FLS920 instrument from Edinburgh Instruments to understand the decay dynamics of excited states. Finally, high-quality sample images and experimental setups were captured using a Canon EOS 5D Mark IV DSLR camera equipped with an EF 24–105 mm f/4L IS II USM lens. These techniques provided a comprehensive analysis of the material properties.

The powder diffraction pattern of YAP NPs undoped and doped with 1–5 mol% Cr3+ at room temperature is depicted in Fig. 2 a. As represented by the standard JCPDS card No. 87–1288, the diffraction peaks in Fig. 2a closely match the orthorhombic YAP phase [44]. Table 3 provides the lattice parameters. The synthesized material retains its crystal structure regardless of the varying levels of Cr3+ ion doping. The position of the peaks in the figure shows that the Cr3+ dopant ions were successfully integrated into the YAP lattice. Regarding CDs/YAP:3Cr3+, a hump peak was discovered at roughly 2θ = 20°, which might be attributed to CDs. The remaining peaks were well indexed to orthorhombic YAP:3Cr3+, suggesting that the crystal form for YAP:3Cr3+ NPs remained unaltered [45] (Fig. 2b). A peak with a typical SiO2 peak was detected at 23° [46]. As seen in Fig. 2b1, the Deconvolution of the XRD peak from 17 to 23° yields two separate peaks corresponding to CDs and SiO2. The larger activated cation substituted the smaller host cation, which resulted in these effects. Therefore, the Bragg's theory (2dSinθ = nλ) further supported these findings. In the YAP matrix, the Al3+ and Cr3+ ions have effective ionic radii of 5.35 Å (CN = 6) and 6.15 Å (CN = 6), respectively.

In the crystal structure, Cr3+ ions are thought to have taken the places of Al3+ ions. A difference of up to 30 % in the ionic radius between host and dopant ions is allowed. The disparity between the dopant ion and substituted ion can be used to assess the extent of substitution. The calculations equation is as follows [47]:
(2)

It is evident from the estimated difference of 14.95 % between the activator and host cations that the activator ions in the current studies were successful in substituting the host ions.

Refinement was carried out to get a better understanding of the dopant ion's crystal structure and doping behaviour in the host YAP matrix. Fig. 2c & d show the refinement profiles of the host and the optimised NPs. The Rietveld refinement patterns of the sample YAP:Cr3+ (1,2,4,5 mol%) are depicted in Fig. S1. Fitting profiles make it very evident that calculated findings accord well with experiment. Table 3 also provides an overview of the refining settings. The synthesised samples appear to have an orthorhombic system with a Pbnm space group, based on the refinement [48]. The residuals (Rp, Rwp) values and χ2 show how good the Rietveld refining method was. Rp and Rwp, respectively, indicate the variation between calculated and experimental values. Throughout the refining process, these two figures can be used to determine how to modify a parameter. Additionally, fitting prominence with an ideal value of one is assessed using χ2. The Rp and Rwp factors, as well as the reduced χ2 for each sample, indicate strong convergence of fit based on the refining results [49]. The optimised sample was shown to have greater unit cell characteristics (a, b, and c) than the host sample (Table 3). This was probably brought on by the smaller host ion being replaced by the bigger dopant ion. Moreover, the Cr3+ doped sample appears to have had a decrease in unit cell volume. All of these results point to the host material having been successfully doped with Cr3+ ions.

Fig. 2e illustrates the crystal structures of YAP:3Cr3+ nanoparticles. In the YAP crystal structure, Y3+ ions are located at dodecahedral sites with a CN of 12, while Al3+ ions occupy octahedral sites with a CN of 6. O can be coordinated in two different ways: YO12 dodecahedra and AlO6 octahedral. There are four formula units with a total of 20 atoms in the traditional unit cell of YAP. A three-dimensional (3D) network of corner-sharing AlO6 octahedra forms the perovskite structure, with Y atoms located in the void spaces between the octahedra [50]. The O atoms are located in the Wyckof positions 4c and 8d, whereas the Y and Al atoms are located in the Wyckof positions 4c and 4b, respectively.

Peak broadening analysis is used in the Debye-Scherrer method to determine the size of the crystallite. Equation (3) is used in this method to depict the average crystallite size [51].

In this case, λ indicates the X-ray wavelength that was employed, β indicates the FWHM value, θ represents the Bragg angle, and k is a constant (0.89). Dislocation density, a metric used to quantify the extent of crystal defects, is defined as the number of dislocation lines per unit volume of the crystal and may be computed for both the host and different doped samples using the average particle size data. Moreover, the degree of crystallinity in the NPs will be disclosed by the dislocation density. The dislocation density was found using using Equation [52]:

The dislocation density and crystallite size as a function of dopant ion concentration are shown in Table S1. The calculation reveals that the NPs in this work have a rather low dislocation density. A high degree of crystallinity in the synthesised nanoparticles is indicated by a low dislocation density.

Size widening and strain broadening need to be paired for the W–H plot. By examining peak width as a function of 2θ, the W–H technique is the most effective integral breadth method for differentiating between strain-dependent line broadening and crystallite size. Equation (6) takes into consideration the isotropic quality of the crystal, or the fact that all parameters are independent of the direction in which they are evaluated. Therefore, it was assumed that the micro strain was constant across all crystallographic orientations. Analysing the orthorhombic phase by plotting the term βCosθ vs. 4Sinθ, the samples under investigation exhibited peak alignment which is supported by Fig. S2. Particle size and strain are indicated, respectively, by the slope and intercept of the linearly fitted plot. Table S2 summarises all of the values that were found to correspond to the crystallite size and micro strain of synthesised NPs.

Fig. S3 presents the FTIR spectra of the synthesized materials. The peak observed at 3478 cm−1 is due to the vibration of hydroxyl groups that are chemically linked. The peak at 1630 cm−1 is attributed to the bending motion of H-O–H (water) molecules. The prominent peaks at 693, 468, and 322 cm−1 are associated with the octahedral coordination of aluminum (AlO6 octahedral units). Specifically, the peak at 693 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching of Al–O bonds in the octahedral structure, while the peaks at 468 and 322 cm−1 are related to the Y–O group [55].

The synthesized CDs show a significant absorption band at 3290 cm−1, which is associated with the stretching vibrations of OH (hydroxyl) and NH (amine) groups, as illustrated in Fig. S3. Additionally, a weaker absorption peak at 2930 cm−1 corresponds to another vibration. The peaks at 1722 cm−1 and 1442 cm−1 are due to the stretching vibrations of O-C=O (carbonyl) and C=C groups, respectively. Moreover, the CH/CN stretching band is present at 1245 cm−1 on the surface of the CDs, and the characteristic amine H-N stretch is observed at 1090 cm−1. The stretching vibrations of C-O, CH2, and C-N are responsible for the IR band at 823 cm−1 [56].

When the FTIR spectra of YAP:3Cr3+ NPs are compared with those where CDs were added to form NCs, similar absorption features are observed. Peaks at 2930, 1722, 1442, 1245, and 1090 cm−1 are clearly identifiable and attributed to the vibrations of the CDs. This indicates that the CDs have been successfully incorporated into the YAP:3Cr3+ NPs.

The notable decrease in the intensity of the –OH group absorption at 3290 cm−1 in the CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ composite, combined with a marked increase in the intensity of the Si-O-Si absorption at 961 cm−1, indicates that the –OH groups on the surface of YAP:3Cr3+ play a role in forming a silica layer. This suggests that the CDs are capable of bonding with the silanol and hydroxyl groups in the silica-coated CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs. Additionally, the emergence of absorption bands around 506 cm−1 is attributed to the rocking vibration of Si-O bonds within the SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs [57].

To further investigate the chemical composition of the YAP, YAP:3Cr3+, CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ and SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NPs, XPS spectra were analysed, with the full-range XPS spectrum presented in Fig. S4a. Fig. S4b presents high-resolution XPS spectra of Y 3d for YAP, YAP:3Cr3+, CDs/YAP:3Cr3+, and SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs. The Y 3d spectra, ranging from 154–160 eV, are deconvoluted into two peaks due to 3d doublet splitting. A peak at ∼156 eV corresponds to Y3+ in a Y-O bonding configuration, while the peak at ∼158 eV represents Y 3d3/2. This trend is consistent across all samples, indicating the presence of Y3+. However, in SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs, a slight binding energy shift towards higher values is observed. This shift suggests changes in the chemical environment, likely due to the influence of SiO2 on the YAP:3Cr3+ matrix. The altered local electronic structure may affect bonding or charge distribution, impacting the composite's overall properties [58].

Further, the XPS spectra of Al 2p were analyzed to explore the chemical composition of YAP, YAP:3Cr3+, CDs/YAP:3Cr3+, and SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs (Fig. S4c). Asymmetric curves observed in both the O 1 s and Al 2p spectra suggest peak splitting, indicating the formation of oxides. The Al 2p peak was deconvoluted into Al 2p1/2 and Al 2p3/2 components, corresponding to spin–orbit splitting. This deconvolution confirms the presence of multiple oxidation states or bonding configurations for aluminum. The binding energy (B.E) of the Al 2p in all the samples is lies between 72.29–72.39 (Al 2p1/2) and 72.69–72.89 (Al 2p3/2). The findings underscore significant role of Al 2p in the formation of oxides, which influences the overall properties of NCs [59]. In the O 1s spectra presented in Fig. S4d, the prominent peak around ∼532.5 eV is attributed to O–H bonds, indicating the presence of hydroxyl groups on the surfaces of YAP NPs. These hydroxyl functionalities enhance the hydrophilicity and reactivity of the materials. Meanwhile, the peak at approximately 531.5 eV corresponds to O-Al bonds, suggesting interactions between oxygen and aluminum, likely indicating the formation of aluminum oxides or hydroxides. An increase in peak intensity for the CDs/YAP:3Cr3+, and SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs suggests a higher concentration of oxygen-related species, which may indicate aggregation of CDs on the NPs. This aggregation could enhance the surface area and reactivity, potentially improving their performance of NCs in applications such as sensing. Furthermore, the O 1s band reveals two additional peaks. One peak at (531.4–531.5) eV is associated with sp2 hybridized carbon (C=O), indicating the presence of carbonyl groups. The second peak at (532.5–532.58) eV corresponds to sp3 hybridized carbon (C-O), pointing to alcohol or ether functional groups. The diversity of these oxygen-containing functional groups suggests a complex surface chemistry that can influence interactions with the environment [60].

The YAP:3Cr3+, CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ and SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NPs show distinct peaks in the Cr 2p spectra at 578.0–578.1 eV (Cr 2p5/2) and 586.8–586.9 eV (Cr 2p3/2). The peak at 578 eV is attributed to Cr3+ ions, corresponding to electrons in the Cr 2p3/2 orbital, indicating the oxidation state of chromium in the material. Additionally, the results suggest that Cr3+ ions adopt an octahedral coordination geometry, which is common for transition metals. This coordination likely occurs as Cr3+ replaces Al in the host matrix, as illustrated in Fig. S4e. Overall, these findings highlight the successful incorporation of chromium into the composite structure [61]. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, shown in Fig. S4f, reveal three primary peaks at 284.2 eV, 285.6 eV and (287.7–288.1) eV. The peak at 284.2 eV corresponds to C–C bonds, specifically sp2 carbon atoms found in aromatic structures. The peak at 285.6 eV is attributed to C–O bonds, indicating the presence of oxygen in hydroxyl groups, which enhance the chemical reactivity of NPs. The peak at (287.7–288.1) eV is associated with C=O bonds, characteristic of carboxyl groups, further emphasizing the oxygen-containing functionalities. The C 1s spectra for SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs are similar to those observed in CDs/YAP:7Cr3+ NCs, indicating consistent surface chemistry [62].

Additionally, the N 1s spectrum was detected exclusively in CDs/YAP:7Cr3+ and SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs within the range of 395–405 eV. The N 1s spectra can be deconvoluted into two peaks: one at 400.6 eV corresponding to amino nitrogen (N–H) and another at 401.6 eV attributed to C-N bonds (Fig. S4g). The presence of nitrogen species indicates the incorporation of amine groups, which can enhance the interactions of NCs with fingerprint constituents. This functionalization may improve the adsorption of biomolecules found in fingerprints, leading to more effective detection and analysis in forensic applications [63]. The observed slight shift of the Y 3d and O 1s binding energies to higher levels is mainly due to the presence of more electronegative Si atoms coating the surface of YAP:3Cr3+, leading to the formation of Y-O-Si bonds and a decrease in electron densities of the Y and O atoms. In the SiO2@CDs/YAP:7Cr3+ NCs, the Si 2p spectra, shown in Fig. S4h, reveal three peaks at 101.2 eV, 102.3 eV, and 103.3 eV, which correspond to Si-C, Si–O, and Si–O–Y bonds, respectively. This confirms the successful formation of SiO2 on the surface of CDs/YAP:7Cr3+ NCs, enhancing the stability and performance of the NCs for various applications [64].

YAP:3Cr3+, CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ and SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs were investigated by Scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Fig. 3 a presents the SEM image of spherical YAP:3Cr3+ nanoparticles, which are approximately 162–194 nm in size. Following the incorporation of CDs, the CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs exhibit a particle size of about 188 nm with an uneven spherical surface (Fig. 3b). An enlarged SEM image of the CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs reveals the grafted CDs on the surface (Fig. 3c). The particle size distribution histogram derived from the SEM images indicates considerable variation in particle size. The particles range from 4–12 nm, with an average diameter of approximately 7.79 nm (Fig. 3d), confirming the aggregation of CDs on the surface of the NPs. To examine the SiO2 coating on the nanoparticles, the SEM image of SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs was analyzed (Fig. 3e). Fig. 3f illustrates the deposition of a silica layer onto the surface of YAP:3Cr3+, accompanied by randomly distributed CDs. This confirms the effective grafting of CDs onto the nanoparticles while preserving their comparable spherical morphology.

The TEM analysis of the optimized YAP:3Cr3+ phosphor is shown in Fig. 3g, which reveals that the particles exhibit agglomerated free with dimensions ranging from 20-70 nm. In addition, average particle distribution histogram is estimated using ImageJ software and average particles size is found to be 40.36 ± 3.1 nm (Fig. 3h). The narrow distribution of particle sizes suggests a controlled synthesis process, leading to consistent particle growth and minimal agglomeration. Fig. 3i shows the TEM image of prepared CDs, with particle sizes appearing in the range of 2–9 nm. The absence of significant agglomeration in the images indicates good dispersibility of the CDs. The average particle size of the CDs was measured to be 5.34 ± 1.49 nm (Fig. 3j). This relatively small size suggests that the CDs possess high surface area-to-volume ratios. Further, TEM analysis of the CDs incorporated into the CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ composite provides clear evidence of successful incorporation. The TEM images reveal that the CDs, with an average size ranging from 4-5.5 nm, are uniformly coated on the surface of the phosphor particles (Fig. 3k). This confirms the effective integration of the CDs into the YAP:3Cr3+ matrix, which is crucial for enhancing the composite’s overall optical properties. The incorporation of CDs onto the phosphor plays a significant role in improving photoluminescence properties through a mechanism known as Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET). In the YAP:3Cr3+ composite, the close proximity of the CDs to the phosphor surface facilitates energy transfer from the CDs to the YAP:3Cr3+ [61]. The CDs, acting as energy donors, can absorb excitation light and transfer energy non-radiatively to the Cr3+ ions within the phosphor. This FRET mechanism leads to enhanced emission intensity and modified luminescence characteristics, potentially increasing the efficiency of the material for optoelectronic and display applications. Furthermore, the presence of CDs contributes to a broad absorption range, which can enhance the excitation of the phosphor. The nanoscale size and high surface area of the CDs provide additional active sites for interaction with light, thus improving the PL properties. The uniform coating of CDs on the phosphor particles ensures effective energy transfer and minimizes losses, which is beneficial for achieving high-performance luminescent materials. Overall, the TEM analysis confirms the successful incorporation of CDs into the YAP:Cr3+ composite [65], and the FRET mechanism plays a crucial role in optimizing the PL properties, making this composite material suitable for advanced applications in lighting, sensing, and display technologies. The TEM image of the SiO2-coated CDs/YAP:Cr3+ nanocomposites clearly reveals the presence of SiO2 layer (1.72–3.06 nm) surrounding the YAP:Cr3+ phosphor (30–50 nm) (Fig. 3l). The silica coating can be distinctly observed as a thin, continuous layer encapsulating the YAP:Cr3+ particles, confirming the successful deposition of SiO2 on the surface. Additionally, the core–shell structure is evident, with the YAP:Cr3+ nanoparticles located at the core and the SiO2 layer acting as the shell. This coating helps to enhance the stability and photoluminescence properties of the nanocomposites by providing surface passivation and preventing agglomeration.

The crystallinity of the YAP:3Cr3+ is shown by the HR-TEM image (Fig. 4 a). To further investigate the crystallinity, HR-TEM imaging combined with fast Fourier transformation (FFT) analysis using GATAN software. The YAP orthorhombic phase is confirmed by the crystalline NPs, which have a distinctive interplanar distance of 0.26 nm, which corresponds to the (1 1 2) plane (Fig. 4a). The lattice fringes of the CDs structure are clearly visible and FFT image of the selected area reveals the crystallinity of the CDs, with an interplanar spacing of 0.22 nm corresponding to the (002) plane (Fig. 4b). This interplanar spacing is consistent with the graphitic structure of carbon. Fig. 4c presents the HR-TEM image of CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs, clearly showing two distinct planes. The FFT pattern of the selected area, corresponding to the (112) and (100) planes in orthorhombic YAP:3Cr3+ NPs, is also displayed in Fig. 4c. The IFFT and line profile analysis estimate interplanar spacings of 0.26 and 0.22 nm for the (112) and (100) planes, respectively, confirming the presence of CDs on the surface of the NPs. The HR-TEM image of SiO2-coated CDs/YAP:3Cr3 + NCs reveals a shell thickness estimated to be around 1.02–1.13 nm (Fig. 4d). The image also confirms the high crystallinity of the YAP:3Cr3+ core, showing characteristic interplanar distances of 0.26 and 0.22 nm, corresponding to the (112) and (100) planes of the YAP orthorhombic phase and the graphitic structure of carbon, respectively. These measured values align well with the line profiles (Fig. 4d) generated using GATAN software [66], further validating the crystallographic properties.

The mechanism for the core shell on CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs is depicted in Fig. 4e. This process involves stirring and drying the mixture, which includes YAP:3Cr3+, CDs, and TEOS (SiO2 precursor), at 80 °C for 12 h. The high concentration of methoxysilane groups in the resultant CDs led to the production of extremely reactive intermediates that linked to silanol and –OH groups. SiO2 encapsulated NPs can be produced by hydrolysing and co-condensing these CDs with precursors of silica, including TEOS. We then developed CDs grafted YAP:3Cr3+ NCs by hydrolysing TEOS. An inorganic silica framework was formed by the hydrolysis and condensation processes of TEOS. As a result, –OH groups will be adsorbed onto the phosphor surface by the hydrated SiO2, which will then start to precipitate and form precipitation nuclei. Subsequently, more SiO2 will precipitate onto these nuclei, causing a continuous layer of silica to develop. Concurrently, the CDs progressively embed themselves in the silica layer that envelops the NPs [67].

The excitation spectra of YAP:3Cr3+ nanoparticles, with emission observed at 678 nm, are presented in Fig. S5. The excitation spectrum of the Cr3+-doped sample features two prominent broad bands, centered around 405 nm and 535 nm. These bands correspond to the Cr3+ transitions occurring within the octahedral lattice sites of the spinel structure. Specifically, the broad band at 535 nm (ν1 ∼ 18,691 cm−1) is attributed to the spin-allowed 4A2g→4T2g transition, while the band at 405 nm (ν2 ∼ 25,000 cm−1) corresponds to the spin-allowed 4A2 g→4T1g transition of Cr3+ ions. Fig. S5b showcases the photoluminescence spectra under 405 nm excitation. The emission spectra across all samples maintain a consistent shape, prominently displaying an intense doublet at 678 nm and 701 nm. This doublet is recognized as the R1 line (701 nm) and R2 line (678 nm), which are characteristic of Cr3+ ions. These features arise from the spin-forbidden 2Eg→4A2g transition of Cr3+ ions in YAP:Cr3+ nanoparticles, with doping concentrations ranging from 1–5 mol% [68,69].

The effect of Cr3+ concentration on the PL intensity of YAP:Cr3+ (1–5 mol%) NPs under 405 nm excitation is depicted in Fig. S5c. The PL intensity demonstrates a distinct trend as the concentration of Cr3 + ions increase. Initially, the PL intensity rises with increasing Cr3 + concentration, reaching a peak at 3 mol%. This initial increase is attributed to the enhanced probability of energy transfer between Cr3 + ions as they become more closely spaced. However, as the doping concentration continues to increase beyond 3 mol%, the distance between Cr3 + ions decrease further, leading to non-radiative energy transfer mechanisms becoming dominant. This results in luminescence quenching, where the emission intensity begins to decline. The phenomenon of concentration quenching explains this behavior, where the emission intensity first increases and then decreases, with the maximum PL intensity observed at a Cr3 + concentration of 3 mol%. The phenomenon of luminescence quenching is attributed to multipolar interaction. According to Dexter's theory, the interaction in YAP:Cr3 + (1–5 mol%) NPs can be expressed as follows [70]:
(8)
I x
= k
[ 1 + β
x
Q 3
]
- 1
In this case, k and β are constants, I stands for emission intensity, and x is the doping concentration of YAP:Cr3+ NPs. The Q value indicates the type of multipole-multipole interaction: Q = 3 corresponds to interactions between nearest neighbour ions, Q = 6 to dipole–dipole interactions, Q = 8 to dipole-quadrupole interactions, and Q = 10 to quadrupole–quadrupole interactions. The plot of log(I/x) versus logx for the nanoparticles is shown in Fig. S5d, with the calculated slope being −2.32. This corresponds to a Q value of 6.96, which is close to 6. Therefore, the findings indicate that, similar to other phosphors, the primary factor causing concentration quenching in the YAP:Cr3+ NPs is dipole–dipole interaction [71]. The findings suggest that the dipole–dipole interactions play a dominant role in limiting the PL intensity at higher Cr3+ concentrations due to non-radiative energy transfer processes.

The excitation-wavelength-dependent PL spectra of the as synthesised CDs are shown in Fig. 5 a, with the emission peak peaking at 440 nm. The observed dependency of the photoluminescence behavior on Cr3+ concentration can be attributed to the lower excitation energy. In this scenario, the lower excitation energy increases the energy associated with the Stokes shift, which is the difference between the absorbed and emitted photon energies. This larger Stokes shift effectively blocks access to the higher emission energy levels. Consequently, the emission is restricted to lower energy states, which directly influences the observed emission intensity and spectral characteristics. This explanation accounts for the concentration-dependent luminescence quenching observed in YAP:Cr3+ nanoparticles [72]. As a result, we can readily modify the emission peak, setting it at 440 nm with an excitation of 360 nm. Under a excitation wavelength of 390 nm, the CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs demonstrated, as expected, both the distinctive emissions of CDs and YAP:3Cr3+ NPs (Fig. 5b). Furthermore, by varying the excitation wavelength, the emission of the CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs may be systematically controlled. This is because, as seen in Fig. 5c, the emission peaks of YAP:3Cr3+ NCs endure unaltered, while the emission peaks of CDs show a regular red-shift. The colour footprints move from (0.371, 0.310) to (0.252, 0.302) across the white light emission region, based on the CIE chromaticity coordinates shown in Fig. 5d. These results show that altering the excitation wavelength can, in fact, result in white light emission. The PL emission properties of the composite, which was developed by adjusting the concentration of CD solution (1, 2, 4, 10, and 20 wt%) in the YAP:3Cr3+ red-emitting material under 390 nm excitation, are depicted in Fig. 5e. The spectra display the distinctive emissions from NPs and CDs. The luminescent intensity of CDs increases progressively with higher concentrations of CDs. It is evident from the normalised emission spectra that the blue emission component has increased relative to the red emission. Consequently, by varying the ratios of red to blue emissions, the luminous colours of the NCs may be changed from red to blue.

In addition, the integrated photoluminescence intensity of the NCs is much higher than that of YAP:3Cr3+ NPs, irrespective of the excitation wavelength employed. The reason for this improvement is the FRET that exists between Cr3+ ions and CDs. Numerous studies have explored the design and construction of CD-based FRET systems, highlighting their significant potential across a range of applications. These systems demonstrate enhanced emission intensity due to FRET between the CDs and Cr3 +. For effective FRET, three critical conditions must be met: First, the donor (CDs) and acceptor (Cr3 +) ions need to be in close proximity, typically within 1–10 nm, as the efficiency of FRET diminishes rapidly with increasing distance. Second, there must be a substantial overlap between the acceptor’s excitation spectrum and the donor’s emission spectrum to ensure that the energy emitted by the donor is absorbed by the acceptor. Third, the transition dipole orientations of the donor and acceptor must be nearly parallel, optimizing the energy transfer by maximizing the overlap between the emission and absorption spectra. Meeting these requirements is essential for harnessing the full potential of CD-based FRET systems in applications such as bioimaging, sensing, and environmental monitoring [73]. The SEM, TEM, and HR-TEM images presented in Figs. 3 and 4 vividly illustrate the close proximity between the acceptor and donor ions, confirming the structural conditions necessary for effective FRET. Furthermore, the excitation spectra of Cr3+ ions extend over a wavelength range of 350–600 nm, while the emission spectrum of the CDs spans from 300 to 600 nm, as depicted in Fig. 5f. This spectral overlap between the excitation range of Cr3+ ions and the emission range of the CDs facilitates efficient energy transfer, meeting the criteria necessary for successful FRET. The alignment of the Cr3+ ions absorption spectrum with the CDs emission spectrum indicates that FRET is occurring between the two. As a result, this energy transfer leads to an enhancement in the emission light from the CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs, demonstrating the effective interaction between the donor and acceptor ions and contributing to the increased overall emission intensity. Potential pathways for energy transfer are depicted in Fig. 5g, which is based on the energy-level diagram of CDs and Cr3+. After reaching a specific energy, the excited electrons in the CDs move from the π energy level to the π* energy level of C. This transition initiates an electronic relaxation process, where electrons shift from C π* to N π* and from N π* to O π*, ultimately reaching the O π* (LUMO) energy level [74]. Through multi-photon relaxation, electrons in the O π* energy level can also be transferred to higher energy levels of Cr3+. These electrons then undergo non-radiative relaxation to the 4T2g energy level of Cr3+. Additionally, CDs can absorb electrons from the 4T2g energy level of Cr3+ and transfer them to the 4A2g energy level through the FRET process. The combined effect of these interactions results in the augmentation of R lines and an increase in the number of electrons in the 4A2g energy level, reflecting the efficiency of the energy transfer and relaxation processes between the CDs and Cr3+ ions [75].

The coated SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs showed a 2.52-fold increase in intensity compared to the uncoated CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs (Fig. 5h). The PL emission intensity of SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs is about 48.73 folds higher than that of YAP:3Cr3+ NPs. The enhancement in PL intensity observed in SiO2-coated NCs is driven by several key factors:

Surface Passivation of CDs and YAP:Cr3+ Nanoparticles: (i) The surfaces of both CDs and YAP:Cr3+ nanoparticles can possess various defects, dangling bonds, or other non-radiative recombination centers. These surface imperfections often serve as trap states, facilitating non-radiative decay pathways that quench the photoluminescence (ii) Silica coating effectively passivates these surface defects by forming a protective layer around the CDs and YAP:Cr3+ nanocomposites. This passivation reduces the number of non-radiative decay channels and thereby increases the radiative recombination probability, leading to enhanced PL. Minimization of Non-Radiative Energy Transfer: In nanocomposites, energy transfer between different components (such as from CDs to YAP:Cr3+) can lead to non-radiative losses. The silica layer introduces a physical separation, reducing unwanted energy transfer between CDs and YAP:Cr3+ nanoparticles. This barrier minimizes non-radiative pathways, thereby enhancing the overall PL efficiency.

Prevention of Aggregation and Improved Dispersion: (i) Nanocomposite materials, such as CDs and YAP:Cr3+ nanoparticles, can aggregate in the absence of a coating. Aggregation often results in PL quenching due to the increased likelihood of energy transfer between aggregated particles, leading to non-radiative decay (ii) Silica coating helps prevent aggregation by providing a uniform barrier around individual particles, maintaining their dispersion in various media and preventing close contact. This separation leads to a reduction in quenching and thus higher PL intensity.

Enhanced Local Field Effects: Silica coating modifies the local dielectric environment surrounding the CDs/YAP:Cr3+ nanocomposites. The contrast in the refractive index between the silica layer and the surrounding medium can enhance the local electromagnetic field around the luminescent particles, thus increasing the radiative transition rates and leading to stronger PL emission. Protection Against Photo-Oxidation: CDs/YAP:Cr3+ nanocomposites can be prone to photo-oxidation, which diminishes their PL over time due to oxidation-induced defects. The silica layer acts as a protective shield, reducing exposure to oxygen and moisture. This protection helps maintain the stability of the luminescent properties by preventing oxidation-induced quenching. Enhanced Energy Transfer Efficiency within the Nanocomposites: For CDs/YAP:Cr3+ nanocomposites, the silica coating can promote more efficient energy transfer from the CDs to the YAP:Cr3+ component. The silica layer may modify the interaction between the donor (carbon dots) and acceptor (YAP:Cr3+), enhancing the energy transfer efficiency and thus increasing the PL output. In conclusion, the PL enhancement observed after silica coating on CDs/YAP:Cr3+ nanocomposites can be attributed to surface passivation, reduction of non-radiative energy transfer, prevention of aggregation, enhanced local field effects, protection against photo-oxidation, and improved energy transfer efficiency within the nanocomposite. These factors collectively contribute to a higher PL intensity and stability of the luminescent properties.

The variation in photoluminescence (PL) intensity for YAP3+, CDs (4 wt%)/YAP:Cr3+, and SiO2@CDs (4 wt%)/YAP:Cr3+ samples demonstrates significant enhancement upon the incorporation of CDs and subsequent silica coating (Fig. 5i).The base sample, YAP:Cr3+, serves as the reference for the PL intensity. When 4 wt% CDs are incorporated into the YAP:Cr3+ matrix, the PL intensity increases by 19.29 % compared to the pure YAP:Cr3+ sample. This enhancement can be attributed to the energy transfer from the CDs to the YAP:Cr3+, leading to increased radiative recombination. This progressive enhancement in PL intensity from YAP:Cr3+ to CDs (4 wt%)/YAP:Cr3+ and then to SiO2@CDs(4 wt%)/YAP:Cr3+, underscores the synergistic effects of carbon dot incorporation and silica coating in improving the luminescent properties of the nanocomposites [76,77].

To confirm energy transfer from CDs to YAP:3Cr3+ in NCs, you can analyse and compare the decay curves of CD luminescence before and after integration into the YAP:3Cr3+ NCs. In analysing the emission decay of CDs doped samples, the parameters I0, τ1, τ2 and A1 and A2 are crucial for understanding the luminescence dynamics. Here, I0 represents the emission intensity at time t = 0 s; while τ1 and τ2 denote the short-lived and long-lived decay times, respectively. Specifically, τ1 (ranging from 1 to 4 ns) is associated with rapid decay processes, and τ2 (spanning 7 to 11 ns) reflects slower decay components. The fitting parameters A1 and A2 correspond to the amplitudes of these decay processes. The emission decay of CD-doped samples is characterized by a double exponential decay, which indicates the presence of two distinct relaxation pathways. This behaviour is reminiscent of what is observed in luminous silicon nanoparticles (Si NPs), where the decay is influenced by both surface and core states. The short-lived component τ1 is typically linked to electronic transitions and interactions at the surface of the CDs, while the long-lived component τ2 is associated with deeper core states.

When energy transfer occurs, the interaction between the donor (CDs) and acceptor (such as YAP:3Cr3+) can lead to a modified decay profile, where the contributions from different relaxation processes are evident. This behaviour highlights the intricate relationship between the various electronic states within the CDs and how these states influence the overall luminescence properties of the material. Determined by equation, the average decay time of CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ and SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs was calculated to be 4.44 and 2.08 ns respectively, which is lower than that of pure CDs, indicating the existence of energy transfer between CDs and YAP:3Cr3+ NPs in both SiO2 coated and uncoated samples. This provides additional evidence for the energy transfer between CDs and Cr3+.

We recorded the luminescent decay curves of YAP:3Cr3+, CDs/YAP:3Cr3+, and SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs to analyse the lifetime of YAP:3Cr3+ (Fig. S6b). There existed a double-exponential equation that fit the decay curves well. According to the results, the decay times were calculated to be 32.84 ms for YAP:3Cr3+, 39.11 ms for CDs/YAP:3Cr3+, and 34.77 ms for SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs. Compared to YAP:3Cr3+, YAP decay time in a silica matrix were shortened. Nevertheless, the decay time of YAP somewhat increased when CDs were added. This alteration is a result of energy being transferred between NPs and CDs. Our findings suggest that there is energy transfer taking place between the YAP:3Cr3+ NPs and the CDs. The calculated average lifetimes for the uncoated CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ and SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs are 39.11 ms and 34.77 ms, respectively. The decrease in lifespan values observed with SiO2 coating suggests that the SiO2 shell is responsible for the increase in the refractive index of the medium surrounding the nanoparticles. Reduced radiative lifetime of Cr3+ ion electronic transitions might be attributed to rise in refractive index [80].

The effect of water treatment on the PL stability of uncoated and SiO2-coated CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs is depicted in Fig. S7a & b. The PL spectra reveal a significant difference in the moisture-resistance stability between the two samples. After prolonged water exposure, the uncoated CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs showed a substantial decrease in PL intensity, retaining only 69.3 % of their original emission intensity (Fig. S7c). In contrast, the SiO2-coated samples maintained 85.2 % of their initial PL intensity after the same treatment, indicating that the SiO2 coating significantly enhances the moisture-resistance stability of the nanocomposites. The improved stability of the SiO2-coated NCs can be attributed to the protective barrier formed by the SiO2 shell, which effectively prevents water molecules from penetrating the surface and interacting with the core material. This barrier reduces the likelihood of surface oxidation and chemical reactions that could otherwise lead to luminescence quenching. The findings are consistent with previous studies by Liu et al. [76] and Zhuang et al. [77], which reported similar improvements in moisture-resistance and stability through SiO2 encapsulation of luminescent nanomaterials. In conclusion, the enhanced moisture resistance of the SiO2-coated CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs demonstrates the effectiveness of the SiO2 shell as a protective layer, offering a robust approach for improving the durability and long-term performance of luminescent nanocomposites in aqueous environments.

The effect of UV treatment on the PL stability of uncoated and SiO2-coated CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs was studied by exposing the samples to an intense UV source (36 mW/cm2) for durations ranging from 0 to 60 min. The PL spectra, presented in Fig. S7d & e, clearly indicate that there were no significant shifts in the peak positions for either the coated or uncoated samples. However, there was a noticeable difference in the rate of PL intensity reduction between them. After 60 min of UV exposure, the uncoated CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs retained only 61.3 % of their original emission intensity. In comparison, the SiO2-coated YAP:3Cr3+ NCs retained a significantly higher intensity of 82.3 % (Fig. S7f). This indicates that the SiO2 shell effectively slows down the rate of UV-induced degradation. The superior stability of the SiO2-coated samples under UV treatment can be attributed to the protective nature of the SiO2 shell, which acts as a physical barrier that reduces the interaction of high-energy UV photons with the core material. This minimizes the occurrence of surface defects and photodegradation processes that can lead to luminescence quenching. As a result, the SiO2-coated CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs exhibit enhanced resistance to UV-induced damage, making them more suitable for applications requiring prolonged exposure to ultraviolet light. Overall, these findings highlight the beneficial role of the SiO2 coating in improving the UV stability of the nanocomposites, thereby extending their potential for use in optical and photonic applications.

The effect of acid treatment on the PL stability of uncoated and SiO2-coated CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs was investigated by immersing the samples in a 1 N HCl solution for 0 to 50 h. The PL spectra, as shown in Fig. S7g & h, reveal distinct differences in the stability of the two types of samples when exposed to an acidic environment. After 50 h of acid treatment, the uncoated CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs retained only 55.2 % of their original PL intensity, indicating significant degradation. In contrast, the SiO2-coated samples maintained a much higher PL intensity, retaining 86.0 % of the original emission (Fig. S7i). This substantial difference demonstrates the enhanced acid resistance provided by the SiO2 coating. The improved acid stability of the SiO2-coated NCs can be attributed to the protective role of the SiO2 shell, which serves as a chemically inert barrier, shielding the core material from direct exposure to the acidic environment. The SiO2 layer effectively prevents protonation and surface etching processes that typically occur in acidic conditions, thereby reducing the degradation and quenching of the luminescence. This protective effect extends the structural and optical integrity of the nanocomposites under harsh chemical conditions. Thus, the SiO2 coating enhances the acid resistance of the CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs, making them more suitable for applications where stability in acidic environments is crucial. The findings support the use of SiO2 encapsulation as an effective strategy for improving the durability of luminescent nanomaterials.

The effect of base treatment on the PL stability of uncoated and SiO2-coated CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs was evaluated by immersing the samples in a 1 N NaOH solution for 0 to 50 h. The PL spectra, presented in Fig. S7j & k, illustrate a significant difference in the base stability between the uncoated and SiO2-coated samples. After 50 h of exposure to the basic environment, the uncoated CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs retained only 62.5 % of their initial PL intensity, indicating considerable degradation. In contrast, the SiO2-coated samples maintained 89.5 % of their original emission intensity (Fig. S7l), demonstrating significantly enhanced resistance to the basic treatment. The improved base stability of the SiO2-coated NCs is due to the protective nature of the SiO2 shell, which acts as a chemically inert and stable barrier, preventing the core material from interacting with hydroxide ions. This protective layer reduces the likelihood of surface hydrolysis and chemical degradation processes that can occur under basic conditions, thus preserving the luminescence properties of the nanocomposites. The SiO2 coating prevents surface reactions that would otherwise lead to structural and optical damage, thereby enhancing the durability of the material in alkaline environments. These results confirm the effectiveness of the SiO2 coating in providing base resistance to CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs, making the composite more suitable for applications where exposure to basic conditions is expected. The findings support the strategy of using SiO2 encapsulation to improve the chemical stability and longevity of luminescent nanomaterials.

Chromaticity coordinates are essential for assessing the colour appearance of light emitted and the potential of phosphor materials [81]. The chromaticity diagram and CIE coordinates, respectively, provided by the CIE can be used to evaluate the emission colour and properties of the phosphor sample. Chromaticity coordinates are related to the light source colour response at each point in the two-dimensional colour space that the CIE diagram depicts. Color purity (CP) is essential for identifying materials with desired optical properties for practical applications. To understand the purity of the emitted light from a phosphor material, it is necessary to calculate the CP. This unitless parameter ranges from 1 % to 100 %. The following equation can be used to measure the CP of the light emitted from the sample [82]:
(12)
Colorpurity =

× 100 %

In this case, (x, y) stands for the CIE coordinates that were obtained from the CIE diagram, and (xd, yd) for the dominant wavelength coordinates. Drawing a horizontal line from the white light point to the CIE reference point indicated on the CIE plot will yield the dominant wavelength coordinates. The coordinates of the equal illuminant point, which are (0.333, 0.333), are represented as (xi, yi). The color purity of the optimized nanoparticles was found to be 99.4 %. Correlated Color Temperature (CCT) is a key photometric property of phosphor materials that indicates the warmth or coolness of the emitted light. A high CCT value (> 4000 K) corresponds to cooler light, while a low CCT value (< 3200 K) represents warmer light. The CCT value can be calculated using McCamy’s formula as follows [83]:
(13) CCT = -437n3 + 3601n2 −6861n + 5514.31

The developed phosphor material produces light in the cooler zone, as observed by the CCT values that are calculated and displayed in Table 4 a. This characteristic makes it suitable for use in various domestic applications. Typically, white-light emission is achieved by combining red-emitting phosphors with commercial blue and green-emitting phosphors. In this study, we developed an efficient white-emitting composite by combining blue-emitting CDs with red-emitting YAP:3Cr3+ NPs. The CIE and CCT diagrams for the synthesized samples are shown in Fig. 6 (a & b). A CIE system with coordinates of (0.1499, 0.0957) places the synthesised CDs in the blue region, and coordinates of (0.7015, 0.2963) place pure YAP:3Cr3+ in the red region. The samples with varying relative concentrations of CDs (1, 2, 4, 10, and 20 wt%) were also analysed. As shown in Table 4b, the colour could be systematically adjusted from white (0.3259, 0.2532) to blue (0.2247, 0.2132) and from purplish-red (0.4828, 0.2761) to purplish-pink (0.4200, 0.2582). Fig. 6c displays pictures of the corresponding phosphors that have been stimulated by a 365 nm UV lamp. To meet certain standards for white emission, the amount of CDs in the NPs can be changed, which will change the colour composition. These optical properties suggest that the CDs (4 wt%)/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs have significant potential for fabricating WLEDs [84]. After coating the composites with SiO2, the chromaticity coordinates of SiO2@CDs(4 wt%)/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs shifted towards the white region (x = 0.3301, y = 0.2598), as confirmed by the luminescent images shown in Fig. 6c. This shift towards white is likely due to the increased intensity of the NCs resulting from the SiO2 coating.

The dual emission property of CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ and SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs makes them promising candidates for temperature sensing. This is due to the fact that the emissions from CDs and YAP:3Cr3+ exhibit various temperature sensitivities. As displayed in Fig. 7 (a & b), the PL spectra of CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ and SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs were recorded from 80 to 320 K in 40 K intervals, with excitation at 390 nm. As temperature rises, the luminescence intensities of the CDs at 440 nm and YAP:3Cr3+ at 678 nm gradually diminish. This is explained by greater temperature-dependent enhanced non-radiative recombination processes that result in thermal quenching of photoluminescence. The emission peak at 678 nm falls little as temperature rises, but the peak at 440 nm decreases more noticeably. The ratio F678/F440, which represents the emission intensity of YAP:3Cr3+ at 678 nm to that of CDs at 440 nm, defines the thermometric parameter. Over the temperature range, a strong linear association with a correlation coefficient of 0.99 is seen, as illustrated in Fig. 7c and d. This relationship can be expressed as follows [85]:

F 440
= 5.48 - 0.0074 T
(16)

In this case, T stands for a temperature between 80 and 320 K. The relative sensitivity for ratiometric luminescent temperature sensing can be computed with the following formula [85]:
(17)

)
