The maximum sensitivity for both CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ and SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs is achieved at 320 K. We can conclude that, the SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs are successfully applied for temperature sensing over the range of 80 to 320 K. Fig. 7e & f show how the relative sensitivity changes with temperature. Table S2 [86–90] presents a comparison of the relative sensitivity of several methods with values documented in the literature.

The synthesized CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ and SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs were mixed with PDMS, cast onto a UV-LED chip, poured into a round mold, and cured [91]. Fig. S8a & b shows the electroluminescence spectra, which exhibit two emission bands: a broad band at 440 nm corresponding to the CDs, and a characteristic band at 678 nm corresponding to the NPs. The CIE diagram of the fabricated W-LEDs is shown in Fig. S8c. The CIE coordinates of the CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ and SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs are (0.29, 0.30) and (0.31, 0.30), respectively, both of which are close to the equal energy point (0.33, 0.33). CCT is a crucial parameter for evaluating the luminescent performance of W-LEDs. Typically, a lamp emitting warm light has a CCT of less than 3200 K, while a lamp emitting cold light has a CCT greater than 4000 K. Based on this, both fabricated W-LEDs can be classified as cool LEDs, with CCT values exceeding 4000 K. The fabricated W-LEDs, both before and after coating, are illustrated in Fig. S8d. CRI (Color Rendering Index) is essential for assessing the performance of W-LEDs. The comparison with commercial W-LEDs (which use blue chips and YAG:Ce3+) highlights the benefits of the packaged W-LEDs. The diagram shows that most special rendering indices (R1–R14) for commercial W-LEDs are lower than those of the prepared W-LEDs, indicating superior colour rendering in the latter. In comparison with CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs, the SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs shows a greater rendering index. The W-LED manufactured using SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs possesses a superior Ra = 94 than the reported W-LEDs listed in Table S3 [92–97]. We could therefore conclude that our SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs are promising candidates for practical W-LEDs.

The application of CDs as effective probes for metal ion detection is well-known. In this study, we investigated the potential of NCs as fluorescent probes for ion detection. Fe3+ ions significantly quenched the fluorescence of the CDs, while other ions showed little to no effect. Due to this selective quenching, NCs can be specifically used for Fe3+ ion detection, as demonstrated in Fig. 8 a. The emissions of YAP:3Cr3+ NPs were largely unaffected by Fe3+ ions; however, the luminescence intensity of the CDs gradually dropped when the concentration of Fe3+ ions spiked. For ratiometric luminescent ion sensing, the intensity ratio of the two emissions is frequently utilised as a parameter. This metric is defined for SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs as the ratio of CD intensity at 440 nm to YAP:3Cr3+ NP intensity at 678 nm (F440/F678).To find out the detection range and sensitivity of SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs with the additive different concentrations of Fe3+ (0–500 µM) were investigated, and corresponding results were depicted in Fig. 8b & c. Fig. 8d shows a highly sensitive Fe3+ sensing mechanism with a sensitivity of 0.2 µM, indicating that small changes in Fe3+ concentration can be reliably measured down to this level. The linear relationship between the fluorescence ratio (F440/F678) and Fe3+ concentration (0–350 µM) suggests a predictable response, with a correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.99) indicating strong linearity. As shown in Fig. 8e, the fluorescence ratio decreases consistently with increasing Fe3+ concentration. The expression models this relationship, confirming accurate Fe3+ detection across the entire concentration range with high sensitivity [98],
(18)
F 440
F 678
= 1.26 - 0.0021 C
Where, C is the concentration, which ranges from 0 to 350 µM. Meanwhile, the limit of detection (LOD) is calculated to be 4.93 μM according to the IUPAC three times standard deviation rule (LOD = 3(σ/s), where σ.is the standard deviation of the blank signals, and s is the slope of the linear calibration curve). Especially, the LOD of prepared CDs is lower than the permitted level of Fe3+ in drinking water (5.357 μM) [99].

The CIE coordinates shift from the blue region to the white region, as shown in Fig. 8f. These results clearly demonstrate that the NCs serve as a reliable and practical probe for Fe3+ ion detection. Despite their abundance, most luminescent probes for Fe3+ detection rely on fluorescence quenching (turn-off) detection. By utilising the ratio of two emissions, ratio-metric fluorescence systems can reduce the impact of fluctuations in probe concentration, excitation, or detection efficiency on fluorescence intensity. Therefore, the dual-emissive SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs probe offers substantial advantages for practical applications. The analytical performance of this method is compared with other known methods for Fe3+ ion detection in Table 2. Fig. 8g shows the mechanism diagram that shows how Fe3+ ions affect the SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs. Numerous peptide bonds (–CO-NH-) are present on the surface of the CDs in the composites, and these bonds engage with Fe3+ ions to form complexes. Through charge or energy transfer mechanisms, these complexes modify the CDs electronic structure and impact exciton distribution, allowing for the non-radiative recombination of excitons. This interaction results fluorescence quenching [100].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+, CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ and YAP:3Cr3+ nano powders
ce:section.ce:para: To check the applicability of YAP:3Cr3+, CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ and SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs for dactyloscopy the FPs of same donor were developed on AL. foil. To generate the sample LFPs, the FP powders were brushed smoothly onto aluminium foil. The pictures of the LFP are developed using DSLR camera. The LFPs visualized by YAP:3Cr3+, CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ and SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs showing complete patterns of papillary ridges can be seen in Fig. 9 (a, b & c). We can observe that the deposition of YAP:3Cr3+ NPs on the fingerprint ridges were not quite good compared to CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ and SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs. But still we can observe the level I and II features in all the FPs [100]. Using ImageJ software, grayscale pixel values were retrieved along the marked lines on FPs to assess each powder's recognition efficiency as show in Fig. 9(a1, b1 & c1). The friction ridges and furrows could be clearly recognized in CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ and SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs. Even though CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs (Fig. 9b1) emitted the strong fluorescence than FP images from YAP:3Cr3+ NPs (Fig. 9a1) showed lower resolution and poor-quality image compared to SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs (Fig. 9c1). The presence of insufficient uniform grayscale pixel values leads to incorrect tolerances in the values of the grayscale beside ridges and grooves of FPs developed using YAP:3Cr3+ NPs. However, Visualization by and SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs is more sensitive than CDs/YAP:3Cr3+, YAP:3Cr3+ NPs. The 3D interactive surface plot of developed FPs were extracted using ImageJ software [101] are shown in Fig. 9(a2-c2). The distribution of SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs is more uniform than that of CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs, which indicates more detail of sharpness, which is in accordance with the experimental images. The sensitivity of SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs for FP detection may be attributed to the abundant carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, as well as the negative charge on the surface of the NCs [102]. The enhanced interaction between FP residues and NC powders, such as SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+, can be attributed to several key factors. The NCs exhibit improved binding characteristics due to both covalent and electrostatic interactions. Specifically, the formation of hydrogen bonds and electrostatic attractions plays a crucial role. The NCs facilitate FP detection by forming hydrogen bonds with the amino acids, proteins, and lipids present in FP residues. Additionally, electrostatic attractions between the oppositely charged regions of the NC surface and the FP residues further enhance binding. A novel interaction mechanism involves the formation of amide bonds between the NCs and FP residues. The amine groups functionalized on the silica nanoparticles react with the negatively charged silica surface, leading to stable amide bond formation [103]. This interaction is driven by the complementary charge interactions between the positively charged amine groups in the FP residues and the negatively charged silica NPs. Due to these enhanced binding characteristics, SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs were selected for further studies aimed at investigating fluorescence intensity, resolution, and adhesiveness. The functionalized NCs improve the overall performance of fluorescence-based assays or FP imaging techniques by optimizing these properties. The synthesized SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs demonstrate superior LFP detection compared to the materials listed in Table S4 [104–112].

Applicability is a critical factor in evaluating fluorescent powder performance. To test this, LFPs were deposited on various substrates coin (non-porous), compact disc (semi-porous), and craft paper (porous) as illustrated in Figures S9(a-c). The white fluorescent SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs were successfully utilized to visualize these LFPs. Developing LFPs on uneven surfaces, like those on coins, presents a particular challenge. Hence, coins were chosen for further testing to assess the suitability of the white fluorescent FP powder. The successful development and observation of features, such as level I and II in the loop region of the LFPs, demonstrated the high quality of FP production, as shown in Fig. S9a1 . Additionally, developing FPs on substrates that exhibit background fluorescence, such as compact discs, posed a challenge. To address this, materials with inherent background fluorescence were selected, and the white fluorescent FP powder was employed to meticulously reveal ridge features, as depicted in Fig. S9b1. This approach ensured effective visualization of FP patterns against varying background conditions. Even though on the surfaces like craft paper where FP residue is found minimal, we can observe the same features in the loop region as on other surfaces (Fig. S9c1). The uniformity of SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs was analysed using interactive 3D plot of FPs using ImageJ [113]. The NCs were uniformly distributed on semi-porous and non-porous surfaces compared to porous surface as shown in Fig. S9(a2–c2). The fluorescence images of the visualized FPs were meticulously analysed using ImageJ, a software commonly employed for image processing and analysis. This analysis focused on evaluating the gray value differences between FP ridges and furrows at specific locations, as illustrated in Fig. S9(a3–c3). The results of this analysis revealed that the white FP powder exhibited excellent performance in developing FP residue images across a variety of substrates, providing clear and contrasting visuals. This effectiveness is attributed to the strong binding properties of the SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs, which interact with FP residues through the formation of amide bonds. Specifically, the negatively charged SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs form stable bonds with the positively charged amine functional groups present on the FP residues. This interaction is crucial for ensuring high-quality development of FP residues on both non-porous and porous substrates. Consequently, these NCs are well-suited for detecting and visualizing FP residues in diverse substrates, making them valuable tools for crime scene investigations where accurate and reliable detection of LFPs is essential.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Micromorphology of level III features of developed fingerprints using SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs
ce:section.ce:para: To authenticate finger-marks, level III microscopic details are crucial. These details include ridge width, as well as the number, diameter, location, and shape of sweat pores in the FPs. For level III analysis, FPs were developed on aluminum foil. The number of sweat pores present on the ridges varies across different areas. On the selected ridges we found 14 sweat pores as displayed in Fig. 10 a. The positions of sweat pores were categorized as C (center), OS (one side open), and BS (both sides open). Among the ridges analyzed, sweat pores were observed in C and OS positions, as shown in Fig. 10b. For further analysis, the inter-spacing of sweat pores was measured using ImageJ, as illustrated in Fig. 10c. The inter-pore spacing ranged from 400 to 600 µm across the ridges examined. Additionally, the area of sweat pores on the selected ridge varied as shown in Fig. 10d and were clearly distinguishable. Additionally, the sweat pores exhibited various shapes including circle, square, oval, rectangle, and triangle, as depicted in Fig. 10e. Consequently, the unique characteristic features obtained from FPs enhanced using SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ significantly aid in individual identification [114].

To find the stability of SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ in FPs detection, we developed the FPs on Al. foil and left in open atmosphere for 90 days. The photographs of FPs taken at different intervals of experiment are shown in Fig. S10a. The pixel profiles of aged FPs over the marked region shows that there is no much luminescence quenching observed. But we can observe non-uniformity in the FP aged up to 90 days which might be due to accumulation of Dust since the FP was left in open atmosphere. But still we can observe the level I-III features on FPs aged up to 90 days similar to day 1 as shown in Fig. S10a1 . To assess the stability of the developed FP images, they were subjected to thermal conditions at 100 °C for 15, 30, and 45 min. Remarkably, the colour of the FP images remained intact, and the contrast was preserved with excellent grayscale values in the pixel profiles (Fig. S10b). In Fig. S10b1 we can observe the stability of FPs after 45 mins thermal treatment in level I, II and III features in comparison with developed FPs before treatment. Furthermore, the developed LFPs were undergone ultra-sonication treatment in water for variable time intervals (5, 10 and 20 mins). The pixel profile shows that there is small quenching observed in the gray scale value of FPs treated for 20 mins (Fig. S10c). In Fig. S10c1 we can observe the stability of FPs after 20 min ultra-sonication treatment in level I, II and III features in comparison with developed FPs before treatment. We can conclude that the developed FPs exhibit thermal stability, as they remain stable and retain their contrast and colour even after exposure to high temperatures [115].

A simple and effective strategy was employed to incorporate CDs into nanoparticles through the hydrolysis of TEOS. The XRD peaks were well-indexed to orthorhombic YAP:3Cr3+ nanoparticles. The SiO2 coating was indicated by a peak at 22° in the PXRD pattern of the NCs, whereas the synthesised CDs showed the distinctive turbostratic structure of the (002) plane in the XRD pattern. The FTIR spectra confirm the incorporation of CDs into the silica matrix of the nanoparticles. The CDs particles are spherical in shape and have a diameter of roughly 7.79 nm, according to XPS measurement of the chemical bonding energy, which also revealed the existence of sp2 carbon and C-O signals. The incorporation of CDs onto the surface of nanoparticles within the silica matrix is clearly visible. HR-TEM analysis reveals that the SiO2 shell has a thickness of 1.13 and 1.02 nm on the surface of the NCs. Upon excitation at 405 nm, the YAP:3Cr3+ nanoparticles exhibit red emission at 678 nm, attributed to the R lines. The CDs demonstrate excitation-dependent photoluminescence characteristics in the region of 400 to 600 nm. By mixing CDs, the luminescence enhancement process is attributed to the energy transfer between CDs and NPs via the FRET mechanism, which results in a nearly 19.29-fold increase in PL intensity. The SiO2 coating on CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NPs enhances the PL intensity by 2.52 folds of without coating. The SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NPs shows an ultimate increase in intensity of 48.73 folds compared to YAP:3Cr3+ NPs. These NCs are used to build a ratiometric fluorescent probe for temperature sensing and ion detection, which offers greater precision than traditional fluorescence quenching techniques. This is achieved by utilising their dual emission features. The emission of SiO2@CDs/YAP:3Cr3+ NCs can be precisely tuned by optimizing CDs concentration (1–20 wt%), highlighting their potential for use in multi-coloured displays and W-LEDs. The formation of FPs on substrates was facilitated by interactions between the FP residue and the amine groups functionalized on the silica particles within the NCs. Specifically, an amide bond forms between the negatively charged silica nanoparticles and the positively charged amine functional groups on the FP residue. The NCs exhibit exceptional level III FP detection capabilities and demonstrate stability under various treatments, making them highly suitable for practical FP analysis and sensing applications.

M. Gagana: Formal analysis, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. B.R. Radha Krushna: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. S.C. Sharma: Supervision. R. Reeta: Methodology. Swati Mishra: Visualization. B. Sargunam: Validation. Priya Josson Akkara: Data curation. K. Suresh Babu: Methodology. H. Nagabhushana: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.
